author: @bonganilukhele8955
text: is it possible to quantify the efficiency of this device? there are claims that it is an over-unity device

author: @ceejayc6502
text: Brilliant! No surprise there. :)  I wonder if there is a calculation for the increased (if any) torque requirement with 2 cans over 1. But this looks like an extremely cost efficient source of heat.

author: @Barskor1
text: You can spin the inner and outer casings counter to each other increasing turbulence and thus friction.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: Cheers mate - but that is one of those easy to say not easy to do suggestions and we are looking at the concept here - but it is something i will do later - still thanks for that

author: @thagrit
text: So are you going to make a radiator and the rest you'd need to use that as a room heater for instance? Also are you interested in hydrosonic pumps/ cavatation devices? I have watched a bit on it but no one ever tells you enough to make your own.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i am indeed and i am looking at it mate

author: @trailkeeper
text: I once seen a vid where someone made a water heater out of some principle of these things.  I think it had like holes in the sides or disks or something, and it was called a cavitation heater or something, basically hot water quickly.  I guess it had a motor spinning it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: He called it a hydrosonic pump i believe
